title: Hai or Hain
subtitle: Which form of 'to be' does this subject take?
tip: Count the subject first, then add respect. One ordinary thing or person takes hai; many of anything, or anyone you would speak to politely, takes hain.

left.label: HAI
bullets: One ordinary person or thing in the third person: woh ladka, yeh kitaab, mera bhai.
A single named person you are not honouring: Raj, Mira, woh kutta.
One item or place: ek pankha, yeh ghar, woh dukaan.
If you can swap in 'is' for a single non-respect subject, use hai.

right.label: HAIN
bullets: Any plural subject: ve log, mere dost, do bachche, yeh kitaaben.
Respectful singular for elders and seniors: aap, papa ji, guru ji.
A named adult shown respect with -ji: Sharma ji, Gupta ji.
Think 'are': both 'they are' and the polite 'you are' take hain.

items.explain: One boy in the third person takes the singular hai.
A single girl takes the singular hai.
One brother, no special respect, takes hai.
One sister takes the singular hai.
A single book is one thing, so hai.
One house takes the singular hai.
Ek marks exactly one, so hai.
A single cat takes hai.
One named person with no respect marker takes hai.
A single named peer takes hai.
One friend takes the singular hai.
A single woman, spoken about plainly, takes hai.
One cup of tea is a single thing, so hai.
One room takes the singular hai.
Ek marks exactly one flower, so hai.
One man spoken about plainly takes hai.
Treated as one mass, saamaan takes hai.
One son takes the singular hai.
A single shop takes hai.
Ek marks one child, so hai.
A single car takes the singular hai.
Ek marks exactly one apple, so hai.
One school takes hai.
A single city takes the singular hai.
One door takes hai.
Log here is plural, so it takes hain.
More than one friend takes the plural hain.
Two children is plural, so hain.
The plural kitaaben takes hain.
Several boys take the plural hain.
More than one sister takes hain.
Paanch marks several flowers, so hain.
Three chairs is plural, so hain.
A group of siblings takes the plural hain.
Several women take the plural hain.
More than one neighbour takes hain.
Paanch marks several mangoes, so hain.
Four friends is plural, so hain.
A group of students takes the plural hain.
Many people is plural, so hain.
The polite aap always takes hain, even for one person.
An elder addressed with -ji takes the respectful hain.
Respect for a parent makes this hain.
A named adult honoured with -ji takes hain.
A teacher shown respect takes the respectful hain.
Grandfather with -ji takes the respectful hain.
A respected named adult takes hain.
Grandmother with -ji takes the respectful hain.
Sahab marks respect, so this takes hain.
A community elder with -ji takes the respectful hain.
